data.title: McKesson Defied The Doubters (Including Me)--And Could Do It Again
data.description:
In the last few quarters, I was not longer bullish about McKesson Corporation (NYSE:MCK) anymore and in the last three articles, I rated the stock as a “Hold”. But for all three articles, the rating can rather be seen as a mistake, and I should have been bullish instead.

Article Published

January 2025

September 2024

April 2024

MCK Performance

19.8%

41.3%

33.4%

S&P Performance

4.9%

14.9%

23.6%

Especially in September 2024, I argued that the correction was not over for McKesson and assumed a rather lowered stock price for the months to come. But looking back, it seems like the correction was almost over when I published the article and McKesson was close to a temporary low, making it a great investment at that point. The stock increased over 40% since September 2024, which was not only a clear outperformance of the S&P 500, but also an impressive return on an absolute basis.

On August 6, 2025, McKesson will report first quarter results for fiscal 2026 and we will look at the company and stock again to determine if McKesson is a good investment before earnings.

Quarterly and Full Year Results

As is often the case in my articles, I start by looking at the last reported results – fourth quarter and full year results for fiscal 2025. Revenue in the fourth quarter increased from $76,355 million in Q4/24 to $90,823 million in Q4/25 – resulting in 18.9% year-over-year growth for the top line. Operating income increased 30.8% year-over-year from $1,216 million in the same quarter last year to $1,591 million in this quarter. And finally, diluted earnings per share increased from $6.02 in Q4/24 to $10.01 in Q4/25 – resulting in 66.3% bottom line growth.

When looking at full-year results, we see similar numbers and high growth rates for the top and bottom line. Revenue increased from $308,951 million in fiscal 2024 to $359,051 million in fiscal 2025 resulting in 16.2% year-over-year growth. Operating income increased 13.1% year-over-year from $3,909 million in fiscal 2024 to $4,422 million in fiscal 2025. Diluted earnings per share grew from $22.39 in the previous year to $25.72 in fiscal 2025 – resulting in 14.9% year-over-year growth. While adjusted earnings per share were almost the same as GAAP earnings per share in the fourth quarter, there is a huge discrepancy for the full year results. Adjusted earnings per share increased from $27.44 in fiscal 2024 to $33.05 in fiscal 2025 resulting in 20.4% year-over-year growth. Finally, free cash flow increased from $3,627 million in fiscal 2024 to $5,226 million in fiscal 2025 – resulting in 44.1% year-over-year growth.

Medical-Surgical Solutions Spin-Off

One of the major news stories was the Medical-Surgical Solution spin-off that McKesson announced during the last earnings call. This is the segment that seems to be struggling the most. When looking at the full-year results, revenue increased only slightly from $11,313 million in fiscal 2024 to $11,386 million in fiscal 2025. Operating profit for the segment declined even 18.8% year-over-year to $773 million.

In the last earnings release, the company wrote:

Today McKesson is announcing its intention to separate its Medical-Surgical Solutions segment into an independent company (“NewCo”). Disciplined portfolio management is a hallmark of our enterprise strategy. McKesson believes a separation will further enhance the strategic opportunity and operational focus of both companies and unlock value for McKesson shareholders. The separation would advance McKesson and NewCo’s ability to create value for customers, partners, patients, and shareholders. With a separation of Medical-Surgical Solutions, McKesson will focus the Company and its capital deployment priorities on opportunities that best align with its long-term enterprise strategies, advancing McKesson’s portfolio to focus investment on higher growth, higher margin opportunities in Oncology and Biopharma Solutions, unlocking substantial value for stakeholders.

During the BofA Securities 2025 Healthcare Conference, CFO Britt Vitalone also commented on the spin-off and was quite optimistic for the “new” McKesson business:

We believe that for the remaining business, the larger McKesson business, focusing on oncology, biopharma services, more discrete focus, more discrete capital allocation against those will drive higher growth, will drive higher returns, and ultimately higher value for our shareholders.

We already mentioned above, that the Medical-Surgical Solution grow only in the very low single digits in fiscal 2025 and for fiscal 2026, expectations are also rather for this segment.

Double-Digit Growth in 2026

McKesson is not providing an outlook for the first quarter, but for fiscal 2026 and management is optimistic its double-digit growth rates will continue in the coming quarters. While the Medical-Surgical Solutions segment will grow only between 2% and 6%, the U.S. Pharmaceutical and Prescription Technology Solutions are expected to grow adjusted operating profit in the double digits.

Combing the segments, McKesson is expecting revenue to increase between 11% and 15% compared to fiscal 2025 and earnings per diluted share are expected to be in a range of $36.75 and $37.55 in the following year. Free cash flow is expected to be a little lower again and in a range of $4.4 billion to $4.6 billion.

Looking at the earnings revision data in the last few quarters, we see that revenue estimates have constantly increased in the last few months. Estimates for earnings per share were also increased a little bit over the last few months, but the lines look rather flat here. When looking at data for the last three months, almost every analyst increased estimates for revenue as well as earnings per share for the upcoming fiscal year.

For the upcoming quarter, analysts expect revenue to be $96.05 billion (which would result in 21% top line growth) and GAAP earnings per share are estimated to be $7.76 (which would result in 10% bottom line growth).

In the upcoming earnings release, we should also look out for updates about McKesson spinning off its Medical-Surgical Solutions business and management might also provide a timeline when investors can expect the next steps. Always an important topic are drug prices in the United States, which are extremely high (in comparison to almost every other country around the world). And lowering drug prices will certainly have an impact on McKesson’s business, but it seems unlikely that the Trump administration will be successful here and lower drug prices in a significant way (similar to previous administrations which also rather failed tackling this issue).

Double-Digit Growth Long-Term

Management and analysts are not only optimistic for the upcoming quarter and fiscal year, but also for the mid-term and long-term. When looking at the expected growth rates in the years to come, analysts are very optimistic and expect earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 10.7% in the years between fiscal 2024 and fiscal 2030.

While analysts are already quite optimistic, management seems even more bullish about its own company. We already mentioned management’s expectations for fiscal 2025 above, but McKesson also is holding on to its long-term growth targets. And long-term, U.S. Pharmaceuticals is expected to grow between 6% and 8% while Prescription Technology Solutions is expected to grow its revenue between 11% and 12% over the long run. In combination with share buybacks, management is expecting adjusted earnings per diluted share to grow within a range of 12% to 14%.

In previous articles, we mentioned several times that share buybacks play an important role for McKesson and the company is allocating a significant portion of its free cash flow to share buybacks. In the last few years, the company spent between $3.0 billion and $3.5 billion on share buybacks and since 2006, when the company started to buy back shares, the number of shares outstanding declined from 319 million to 126 million – resulting in a CAGR of 4.8% and share buybacks therefore make a huge contribution to bottom line growth.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

A final and important step is to determine an intrinsic value for the stock and answer the question if a stock is over- or undervalued. First hints whether to be bullish or bearish about a stock often come from simple valuation multiples like the price-earnings ratio or the price-free-cash-flow ratio. At the time of writing, McKesson is trading for 27.5 times earnings and for 17.4 times free cash flow.

Both metrics are above the 5-year average right now, and while the P/E ratio is not indicating a bargain at this point, trading for 17.4 times free cash flow is making McKesson not really expensive at this point.

And when using a discount cash flow calculation, we also come to the conclusion that McKesson is rather a bargain right now. As always, we are calculating with a 10% discount rate and the last reported number of diluted shares outstanding, which was 125.9 million. As basis for our calculation, we can use the free cash flow of the last four quarters, which was $5,226 million. And let’s be optimistic and assume 10% growth for the next ten years (in line with analysts’ assumptions) followed by 4% growth till perpetuity. When calculating with these assumptions, we get an intrinsic value of $1,031 for the stock and it is therefore still undervalued.

It seems like the free cash flow of the last four quarters is a reasonable basis for our calculation. In the last four quarters, 1.5% of revenue ended up as free cash flow and this is also the free cash flow margin of the last 10 years.

4% growth till perpetuity also seems like a reasonable assumption, but we might question if 10% growth for the next ten years is reasonable. When looking at the last ten years, McKesson grew its earnings per share with a CAGR of 15.16% and therefore 10% annual growth certainly seem reasonable. Management’s own long-term targets also higher.

However, we can also argue to use the midpoint of the company’s guidance for free cash flow, which is $4.6 billion. All other assumptions remaining the same, this would result in an intrinsic value of $908 and the stock would still be undervalued.

Conclusion

It seems like I have been too pessimistic in the last few quarters and I probably should have remained bullish about McKesson as an investment. But it is always easy to look back at the stock performance and know in hindsight what would have been the best move. And when now changing the rating to bullish, there is the major risk of trying to jump on the train after it already left the station. After all, McKesson increased about 600% since the lows in 2018 and at such a point a correction seems likely.

Nevertheless, we still can make the argument that McKesson is most likely still trading below its intrinsic value and despite using lower growth rates than those projected by management in its long-term guidance. Although I might be too late to the party now, I will change my rating to a “Buy” and for a long-term investor, McKesson is most likely a good investment (as long as we are prepared for setbacks).

data.title: Treasury Market Sees No Rate Cut By Sept., 30-Year Treasury Yield Near 5%: The Yield Curve And 'Real' Mortgage Rates
data.description:
The 6-month Treasury yield, which is a good indication of rate hikes and rate cuts by the Fed within 2-3 months, is still glued to the underside of the Effective Federal Funds Rate (EFFR), which the Fed targets with its policy interest rates, and is thereby not yet predicting rate cuts within its vision of 2-3 months.

Last year, on June 26, the 6-month Treasury yield (then 5.33%, matching the EFFR) started skidding in anticipation of the 50-basis point rate cut that the Fed announced on September 18. By July 25, so exactly a year ago, it had dropped by 18 basis points to 5.15%. By August 28, it had dropped by 50 basis points to 4.83%, having fully priced in the 50-basis point rate cut.

Today, July 25, the 6-month yield is still glued to the underside of the EFFR, and so it has still not begun to price in a rate cut at the September meeting, despite the public spectacle of Trump keelhauling Powell on a daily basis to get the Fed to cut its policy rates.

This 6-month Treasury yield is a summary depiction of the short end of the bond market where traders and algos look at millions of data points and at everything the Fed says and does not say to take bets on what will happen with interest rates over the next few months. And it still says, nothing will happen.

At the long end, the bond market is nervous.

The 30-year Treasury yield spent all week in the 4.90% to 4.96% range. On Friday, it ended at 4.92%, 59 basis points above the EFFR. Last week, it was at 5.0% for four days. So far in July, it has risen by 14 basis points.

Since the Fed cut by 100 basis points starting in September (dotted blue line), the 30-year yield (red line) has risen by 99 basis points! That was a massive move by the bond market against the Fed. And it has taught the Fed a lesson about spooking the bond market. It has since then switched to wait-and-see.

In this inflationary environment, the secret question is: How many rate cuts would it take to spook the bond market into pushing the 30-year yield to 6%?

The government has been trying actively to push down long-term yields, or at least keep them from rising further. It said that it would only slowly replenish its checking account, the Treasury General Account – which had been partially drained during the debt-ceiling period – by increasing the issuance of short-term Treasury bills and taking it easy with the issuance of long-term notes and bonds.

Since mid-2024, the government has also been engaging in buybacks, where it issues new debt and uses the proceeds to buy back Treasury securities that had been issued some time ago. Some of the buybacks of long-dated securities – such as 30-year bonds issued in 2020 through mid-2021 with coupon interest below 2% – occur at massive discounts, whereby investors lock in their losses and the government reduces its outstanding debt. This is another effort, initially engineered by the Yellen-Treasury and continued by the Bessent-Treasury, to push down long-term yields.

The 10-year yield ended Friday at 4.39%, just above the EFFR. Despite some ups and downs, it hasn’t gone anywhere over the past five months.

The bond market is nervous about inflation. Inflation, enemy #1 of holders of long-term Treasury bonds, has been accelerating, driven by inflation in services. And the bond market is nervous about a lackadaisical Fed in light of this or potential future inflation.

And it’s nervous about the swelling Mississippi River of new Treasury debt flowing toward the bond market that it has to absorb, possibly with higher yields to draw in more buyers. Higher yields mean lower bond prices; that’s attractive for future bond buyers, and that’s what it takes to pull more buyers into the market to absorb the Mississippi River of new debt. And it means more bloodletting for existing bondholders.

But short-term yields up to six months haven’t budged much and remain glued to the EFFR of 4.33% as rate cuts are still on ice.

Expectations of rate cuts further down the road have pushed down yields over six months and into the five-year range.

The yield curve chart below shows the yield curve of Treasury yields across the maturity spectrum, from 1 month to 30 years, on three key dates:

  • Red: Friday, July 25, 2025.
  • Gold: January 10, 2025, just before the Fed officially pivoted to wait-and-see.
  • Blue: September 16, 2024, just before the Fed’s rate cuts started.

The nervousness at the long end, rate cuts on ice at the short end, and rate-cut expectations for the next couple of years caused the yield curve to sag in the middle.

“Real” mortgage rates are back to pre-QE normal.

Mortgage rates of government-backed 30-year fixed mortgages run roughly in parallel with the 10-year Treasury yield, but are higher, and that spread varies.

In the latest reporting week, the average 30-year fixed mortgage rate ticked down to 6.74%. It has been in that range, near 7%, all year.

The super-low mortgage rates during the Fed’s mega-QE – when it bought trillions of dollars of MBS – were an anomaly of history that has caused the biggest explosion of home prices ever, along with raging inflation, into mid-2022. What’s left over now is a massive hangover, huge housing debt, and a frozen housing market.

“Real” mortgage rates – mortgage rates adjusted for inflation – have been back in the pre-QE normal range since the second half of 2023: The average 30-year fixed mortgage rate minus CPI inflation was 4.15% in June (average mortgage rate for June of 6.82% minus CPI for June of 2.67%).

The home-price explosion – home prices spiked by 50% and more in a couple of years – occurred from mid-2020 through mid-2022, driven by deeply negative “real” mortgage rates, when raging inflation far outran the Fed-repressed 3% mortgage rates. Borrowing at a 30-year-fixed rate of 3% while inflation was far higher was better than free money, and people went nuts because price suddenly didn’t matter anymore because money was better than free. This episode has caused this Fed to go down in WOLF STREET history as the “the most reckless Fed ever.”

Since then, the Fed has backed out of this recklessness, and “real” mortgage rates are back to normal, but home prices are way too high, and the housing market is suffering from a massive hangover.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 07/26/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: PML: Discounted Valuation As A Result Of Weak Performance
data.description:
Overview

PIMCO Municipal Income Fund II (NYSE:PML) operates as a closed end fund that aims to provide a diverse exposure to municipal bonds that can provide investors with income that is exempt from federal income tax. The fund specifically focuses on holdings that have strong credit ratings, which makes PML a low risk value proposition for investors. However, a fund like PML is best utilized by investors that are nearing or at the retirement age. PML has an inception dating back to 2002, which means we have over two decades worth of operating history to reference. Additionally, the fund sports a management fee of 0.69%.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see how PML's share price has declined by a little more than 15%. Even when including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return still amounts to a loss of 10%. PML currently offers a starting dividend yield of 6.5%, and the distributions are paid out on a monthly basis. This can make the fund an enticing choice for investors looking to create a stream of dividend income with their portfolio. However, the dividend coverage has been a bit weak in recent years, so I remain cautious about the possibility of a cut.

Due to the fund's exposure to bonds and the utilization of leverage, PML has demonstrated a clear vulnerability to the current interest rate environment. I believe that higher rates have caused the fund's share price to remain heavily suppressed over the last few years. This could present an attractive entry opportunity for investors that remain optimistic about the possibility of future interest rate cuts. A lower interest rate would likely serve as a positive growth catalyst for PML. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that the fund implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy & Risk Profile

According to the latest fact sheet, PML has total managed assets of $932.8M that is spread across a diverse range of states and sectors. The fund spreads its assets across different municipal bond sub-sectors as a way to mitigate risk and provide a sense of income stability. The fund aims to invest at least 90% of its assets into municipal bonds whose interest is federally tax exempt. The fund spreads its investments across different states, with the top exposure areas being:

  • Texas: 14.7%
  • New York: 9.9%
  • Illinois: 8.2%
  • Arizona: 5.5%
  • Other: 12.2%

Looking at the breakdown below, we can see that PML is most heavily allocated to Hospital Revenue, which accounts for 16.9% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to Industrial Development and Transportation, which account for 13.6% and 11.7% of assets respectively. The combination of these three focus areas makes up more than 42% of total assets, so we can assume these will have the greatest impact on PML's daily price change and total return potential.

Additionally, PML aims to invest at least 80% of its assets into holdings that have strong credit ratings and are investment grade. For reference, ratings that are BBB and above are considered to be investment grade. These are typically the strongest type of borrowers that have the highest probability of paying back debt. The breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 95.65%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 2.14%
  • Not Rated: 2.16%

Since the fund's credit profile is so strong, I have no worries about the possibility of defaults. However, I believe the primary source of risk for PML is the heavy use of leverage that is deployed in an effort to amplify returns. The total effective leverage currently sits at 42.38% of assets, which is extremely aggressive in this higher interest rate environment. Investors must consider the risk that leverage can also amplify losses if allocated towards investments that don't perform as anticipated.

A period of higher interest rates, means that it is more costly to use debt. Therefore, the spread between PML's cost of leverage and the yield collected from securities becomes thinner. This makes it more challenging for the leverage to be used efficiently, which can negatively impact earnings and keep valuations suppressed. Just to demonstrate, the chart below shows the clear inverse relationship. We can see how PML's price rapidly increased throughout 2020 and 2021 when interest rates were cut to near zero levels in response to the pandemic. Conversely, PML's price rapidly declined throughout 2022 and 2023 as interest rates were hiked to their decade highs. Since rates still remain near those highs, PML's share price has stayed suppressed.

Therefore, there is the risk that interest rate cuts do not occur for the next few quarters and PML's price remains suppressed. The Fed has kept rates unchanged for the fourth straight meeting as they continue to monitor macroeconomic data such as unemployment, inflation rates, and shifts in consumer spending. Additionally, the full impact of President Trump's tariffs is still to be determined, so the Fed does not want to cut interest rates prematurely.

Valuation

We can conclude that a lower interest rate environment will serve as a positive growth catalyst for PML. The only issue is that we don't necessarily know when rate cuts will finally be implemented. However, opportunistic investors can use this uncertainty to their advantage. Since PML operates as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying assets. As a result, PML currently trades at a discount to NAV of about 4.8%. Looking back over the last ten year history below, we can see that this is the most attractive discount level experienced in a decade. If you are optimistic about interest rates being cut at some point over the next twelve months, this could be a great entry point.

To get better insights into the internal performance of the fund, I reviewed the latest annual report for 2024. Net investment income amounted to $0.36 per share, while net realized losses were $0.35 per share. Looking back on prior years, we can see that net investment income levels have consistently declined over recent years. As mentioned, this can be attributed to the lower spread between the fund's cost of leverage and the yield collected from securities. While interest rates remain elevated, I anticipate that PML's net investment income levels will remain suppressed.

Additionally, the higher interest rate environment has made it difficult for the fund to capture positive net realized gains. The poor performance has ultimately led to the fund's net asset value declining over recent years. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2020: $12.42 per share
  • 2021: $12.37 per share
  • 2022: $8.76 per share
  • 2023: $9.01 per share
  • 2024: $8.56 per share

The higher interest rate environment has made it extremely difficult for the fund to generate positive returns. So I want to make it very clear that although the valuation is attractive right now, this is due to the poor performance. As long as rates remain high, PML will continue to underperform, so if investors start a position too soon, invested capital may continue to be eroded.

The Dividend

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.0395 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 6.5%. If we multiply this monthly payout amount by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout amount of $0.474 per share. However, the recent earnings indicate that the fund hasn't actually generated enough income to support the distributions. Referring to the same annual report, here are the annual earnings amounts:

  • 2020: ($0.05) per share
  • 2021: $0.00 per share
  • 2022: ($0.10) per share
  • 2023: ($0.25) per share
  • 2024: ($0.06) per share

As we can see, the fund has basically reported losses for five consecutive years. As a result, the dividend payouts have consistently declined over the last ten year period. Therefore, it becomes very difficult to recommend PML for investors that are looking for a consistent source of dividend income. The whole point of maintaining exposure to high-yielding assets, is for the income they can provide. If that income is constantly under threat of reduction, it defeats the purpose of even holding a position.

Since the income is derived from municipal bond interest, the dividends received are exempt from federal income taxes. However, you may still have to pay state taxes on the distributions received. Additionally, the distributions seem to be coming from a combination of net investment income and return of capital. If you want to completely minimize the potential tax implications, PML would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I do think that PML trades at an attractive valuation, but this is due to the poor underlying performance. The fund's heavy use of leverage has kept performance and valuations suppressed during this period of elevated interest rates. A higher interest rate makes it more difficult for leverage to be used efficiently as the fund's operating spread gets thinner. If you are optimistic about the outlook of interest rate cuts, this may be a solid time for entry. However, the fund's internal performance gives me a low level of confidence that PML will remain resilient in a scenario where interest rate cuts are delayed.

data.title: Tutor Perini: Growing Backlog, Expanding Margins, Reasonable Valuation
data.description:
One of the industries that has been outperforming the broader market in 2025 is Construction & Engineering. I covered a few stocks in this industry over the past few years including Sterling Infrastructure (STRL), Limbach Holdings (LMB), Fluor (FLR), Emcor Group (EME), MasTec (MTZ), and Comfort Systems USA (FIX). In this article I'm beginning coverage of Tutor Perini (NYSE:TPC). TPC has multiple positives going for it including a reasonable valuation, strong expected revenue and earnings growth, and a steady flow of contracts to keep the company thriving.

Tutor Perini's Company Background

Tutor Perini operates as a global $2.6 billion market cap construction company, providing general contracting, construction, and design-build services for public agencies and private customers. The company operates and reports financial information under three segments.

The Civil segment comprised 49% of total revenue in 2024. The Civil segment handles infrastructure projects, public works construction, and contracting services. This includes construction and upgrades of bridges, roads, tunnels, military facilities, mass-transit systems, and water/wastewater facilities.

The Building segment comprised 37% of total 2024 revenue. This segment provides a variety of services for specialty buildings in healthcare, gaming/hospitality, commercial offices, transportation, education, sports/entertainment, technology, government facilities, correctional facilities, biotech/pharma, and industrial facilities.

The Specialty Contractors segment comprised the remaining 14% of total 2024 revenue. This segment provides electrical, HVAC, mechanical, plumbing, and fire protection systems for settings such as gaming/hospitality, commercial, industrial, and mass-transit. This segment also provides project management and pre-construction planning, concrete forming/placement, and steel erection.

Tutor Perini's Growth Catalysts

One of the main catalysts for TPC is the steady flow of contracts that the company has been awarded. The company's backlog increased 94% in Q1 2025 to a record $19.4 billion. This growth demonstrates how Tutor Perini is a go-to company for many important construction projects.

The company was awarded a $220 million contract on July 2 from the Tule River Gaming Authority for the Eagle Mountain Casino Phase 2 Expansion. Construction for this project is expected to begin in the summer of 2025 with an expected completion in 2027.

On June 30, 2025, Tutor Perini was awarded a $1.87 billion contract from the Port Authority of New York and New Jersey's Board of Commissioners for the construction of a seven floor steel storage facility and ramp structure components of the Midtown Bus Terminal Redevelopment program in New York City. This will accommodate indoor storage for up to 350 buses. It will also serve as a temporary bus terminal during the construction of a newer bus terminal. The work for this project is expected to begin in the fall of 2025 with the expected completion in late 2028. The contract value will be added to TPC's backlog in Q2 2025.

Tutor Perini's subsidiary, Lunda Construction, received a $34 million contract on June 16, 2025 for the construction of 14 cast-in-place retaining walls and four bridges along Highway 169 in Jordan, Minnesota. Work has already begun on the project with the expected completion to be in November 2027.

On June 12, 2025, Lunda Construction received a $67 million contract from the Minnesota Department of Transportation for the I-94 and I-394 Interchange Project in Minneapolis. This includes the repair of 34 bridges and ramps along these highways. This project is expected to begin in July 2025, with the expected completion in the summer of 2027.

TPC's subsidiary, Fisk Electric, received a $99 million contract on April 30, 2025 for the 2nd phase of the new Harris Health Hospital project. This was added to Tutor Perini's backlog in Q1 2025. The work includes completion of the electrical and fire alarm components for the new hospital tower and podium. Work for this project began earlier this year with an expected completion in Q4 2028.

Those are just the recent examples of the lucrative contracts that Tutor Perini has been awarded. The contracts that I mentioned that were awarded in June and July added to TPC's record backlog of $19.4 billion from the end of Q1.

The company's steady stream of contracts that have been awarded helped drive the 19% increase in revenue to $1.25 billion and the 34% increase in operating income to $65 million that TPC achieved in Q1 2025.

Another catalyst for Tutor Perini is expanding margins. The operating margin increased from 4.7% in Q1 2024 to 5.3% in Q3 2025. The net income margin increased from 1.5% to 2.25% over the same time. TPC expects to achieve further margin gains as the year progresses, especially in the Building and Specialty Contractors segments.

For Q1 2025, the Civil segment achieved the highest operating margin at 13%, while the Building segment had 2.3%, and the Specialty Contractors segment had a negative 4%. The company's largest segment in terms of revenue has the widest operating margin.

Tutor Perini's backlog has been comprised of contracts with higher margins and improved terms. As a result, TPC expects to achieve double-digit revenue growth with strong earnings for the foreseeable future. This should help drive the stock for strong gains.

Valuation

Tutor Perini is trading with a reasonable forward EV/EBITDA of 9.2 which is lower than the sector median forward EV/EBITDA of 12. The stock is also trading with an attractive price/sales of 0.50 as compared to the sector median of 1.6. TPC's reasonable valuation should allow the stock to continue to rise as revenue and earnings continue to grow at a strong pace.

Analysts are expecting TPC to achieve EPS of $1.81 for 2025. This would be a significant improvement over the negative $2.86 diluted normalized EPS that the company reported for 2024. This significant improvement can be driven by the 18% expected growth in revenue to $5.12 billion for 2025 along with continued operating margin increases that TPC has been achieving.

Technical Perspective

It looks like the stock pulled back a bit after reaching its 52-week high of about $52. The purple RSI line (middle of the chart) was in the overbought area (above 70) for a few months before the stock pulled back. The RSI remains in bullish territory above the 50 level, but it is no longer in overbought territory. The MACD (bottom of the chart) turned bearish as the blue MACD line crossed below the red signal line.

So far, this just looks like a consolidation period after the strong run-up over the past few months. The stock could continue to drift lower in the near term. However, with Tutor Perini experiencing many positives and strong growth, I would expect the stock to continue higher over the long-term.

Near-term positive catalysts for the stock include any news of new lucrative contract awards and the next earnings report which is scheduled for August 7, 2025.

Risks for the Stock

Tutor Perini is involved in numerous legal proceedings. Disputes can arise due to unexpected project delays or changes in costs for projects, changes in scheduling, etc. If one or more of these are found to be unfavorable to TPC, it could negatively impact the company's reputation and its revenue/earnings.

The company is at risk for changes in government laws and regulations. This could include tariffs, taxes, or other legislation which could make doing business more expensive for the company. These regulations could increase the cost of supplies, materials, or the costs to perform construction.

Disputes may arise between TPC and its customers. This can occur because sometimes extra work is required that goes beyond what the original contract called for. Disputes may arise due to the required extra costs or other terms associated with the extra work.

Tutor Perini's Long-Term Outlook

Tutor Perini's growing record backlog is poised to drive strong long-term multiple-year double-digit revenue and earnings growth. With a reasonable valuation level, the stock has a chance to outperform the S&P 500 (SPY). TPC's expected revenue growth of 18% for 2025 is significantly higher than analysts' expectations for 9.6% revenue growth for the S&P 500 this year. With that in mind, I expect TPC to outperform at least through the remainder of the year and probably longer.

data.title: VSS: Why I Keeep Buying International Small Caps
data.description:
Hunting for alpha? Have you taken a look at international small caps? The Vanguard FTSE All-World ex-US Small-Cap Index Fund ETF Shares (NYSEARCA:VSS) is up 14 of the past 16 weeks. An incredible rally, VSS is +34% from its April low, outperforming both the S&P 500 ETF (SPY) and the Vanguard FTSE Developed Markets ETF (VEA). The foreign small-cap product has also topped the Vanguard Emerging Markets ETF (VWO) by more than four percentage points.

I have a sizable position in VSS. It’s a long-term holding for me, but I have never covered it on Seeking Alpha. I have a buy rating—not because I own it, but given its strong momentum and attractive valuation. Let’s get to it.

VSS Beating Other Broad-Market Index ETFs Last 6 Months

According to the issuer, VSS provides a convenient way to get broad exposure across developed and emerging non-U.S. small-cap equity markets around the world. It aims to track the performance of the FTSE Global Small Cap ex US Index.

VSS is a medium-sized ETF with $11.4 billion in assets under management as of July 24, 2025. Its annual expense ratio is very low at just eight basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is high at 2.8%. I hold the fund in both taxable and tax-advantaged accounts. Though the yield is high, investors can capture the Foreign Tax Credit by holding it in a regular brokerage account, given the high yield.

Share-price momentum is just about the best it has ever been, at an A- ETF Grade from Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. VSS also scores well regarding risk, thanks to its diversified portfolio and somewhat tame historical standard deviation trends. Finally, liquidity is generally healthy, although I often wait until the final hour of trading each day when I purchase the fund, since the bid/ask spread tends to be quite tight then.

Looking closer at the portfolio, the 3-star, Gold-rated ETF by Morningstar is a rare international fund with virtually no large-cap exposure. Moreover, there a nice mix of small- and mid-cap stocks, per Morningstar’s classification. So, it’s not as speculative as you might think. Finally, value, blend, and growth are all well represented in the allocation.

From a valuation perspective, VSS trades at a very compelling 13.2x price-to-earnings ratio. Considering its high 16.6% long-term EPS growth rate, it’s hard not to think the fund will deliver competitive returns in the next decade.

VSS: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

As for the sector exposure, there’s also a nice mix of positions. Industrials is the largest weight, while Information Technology is 12% of the ETF. Financials is not as big a stake as you might imagine, which helps to offset bank-heavy ex-US products.

Communication Services is a relative underweight to the S&P 500, as is Consumer Staples. Real Estate, which has dramatically underperformed in the past five years, is an overweight, along with Materials.

VSS: Holdings & Dividend Information

By now, you might think a “Strong Buy” is in the cards, but seasonal headwinds temper my near-term expectations. Over the past 10 years, the August through October stretch has been bearish. So, after a tremendous rally in the past three-plus months, a consolidation would certainly make sense.

VSS: Bearish Short-Run Seasonality

The Technical Take

With a low valuation, high EPS growth, YTD alpha, and bearish calendar trends, VSS’s technical chart is strong. Notice in the graph below that shares are within earshot of the 2021 all-time high. Of course, on a total return basis, VSS is already in uncharted territory. I also like that the RSI momentum oscillator has confirmed the price advance, while the long-term 200-day moving average is on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend.

Also take a look at the volume by price profile on the left side of the graph—there’s not much overhead supply of shares to prevent the bulls here. I see support down at the previous breakout level of $126-$127, while resistance is near $142.

VSS: All-Time High In Sight, $142 Support

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on VSS. It’s about 12% of my overall portfolio (my entire net worth), and after a long period of underperformance to the US stock market, I expect strong returns over the decade ahead. Hot 2025 momentum is a great start.

data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook - U.S. Data Dump, Earnings Season And Trade Deals
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Week in review: Trade Deals Materialize

The August 1 tariff deadline approaches, and with it we have had a few trade deal announcements that came out this week. Market sentiment seemed to get a boost, with gold in particular feeling the heat of a stronger US dollar.

Market participants remain at least partially on the edge of their seats, as we have not seen any details of agreements as yet. This led to early signs of cracks in potential trade deals with the US announcing a Japan trade deal that included significant investments in the US.

However, Japanese officials and US officials seem to have differing views of the deal, with Japanese officials stating that the US will secure only 90 percent of profits from joint investments with Japan if it takes on a proportional amount of risk and financing. This seems to suggest that cracks may be present in the two allies’ interpretation of their hastily agreed trade deal.

Japanese officials further stressed there was no written agreement with Washington and no legally binding one would be drawn up after Trump administration officials claimed Tokyo would back investments in the US from which American taxpayers would reap nine-tenths of the profits.

Wall Street and the dollar strengthened on Friday as investors prepared for the upcoming week. All three major indexes were slightly up in early trading and set to end the week with gains.

The week also saw global investors snap up a net $8.71 billion worth of equity funds during the week, reversing a $4.4 billion net withdrawal in the prior week, data from LSEG Lipper showed.

With just a week left before Trump's trade deadline, the US and its partners are rushing to finalize deals. European negotiators are optimistic after the trade agreement with Japan earlier this week.

The dollar strengthened but is still set for its biggest monthly drop as investors focus on upcoming trade talks and central bank meetings. The dollar index rose 0.28% to 97.72, while the euro fell 0.2% to $1.173. Against the yen, the dollar gained 0.4%, reaching 147.57.

In cryptocurrencies, bitcoin dropped 3.08% to $115,133.22, and Ethereum fell 2.63% to $3,641.43.

Oil prices dipped as investors considered global demand and a possible supply increase from Venezuela. US crude fell 0.56% to $65.63 per barrel, and Brent dropped 0.39% to $68.91.

Gold prices also declined as the stronger dollar and optimism over US-EU trade talks reduced demand for the safe-haven metal. Spot gold fell 0.93% to $3,336.52 an ounce.

Earnings Season

Over a third of S&P 500 companies have reported earnings, with 80% beating expectations, according to LSEG data. Analysts now predict second quarter earnings will grow 7.7% compared to the 5.8% estimate from July 1.

Next week, four big tech companies Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), Meta (META), and Microsoft (MSFT) will release their earnings. Investors will closely watch their updates to see if spending on AI is delivering results and if trade tariffs are still affecting their future plans.

The Week Ahead: US Very Much in Focus, Fed Decision, Trade Deals and Earnings

The week ahead has several important data releases lined up. The US and UK will release inflation data with key GDP data from China and manufacturing data from Japan.

Asia Pacific Markets - US/China Trade Talks

The key focus this week is the US-China trade talks in Sweden. A 90-day tariff ceasefire, which started in May, is set to end on August 12. Markets are watching closely to see if the ceasefire will be extended or if there will be changes to current tariffs. An agreement is expected, but uncertainty remains despite President Trump's claims that a framework is in place.

On the data front, China's official July PMI (out Thursday) is expected to stay in contraction at 49.6. The S&P PMI (focused on private and export-driven firms) will follow on Friday. Over the weekend, June industrial profits data will be released. After a sharp drop in May, markets are eager to see if profits recover due to eased trade tensions or if the decline continues.

The Bank of Japan (BoJ) is not expected to make any changes at its meeting on July 30-31. However, markets will pay attention to the BoJ's updated economic outlook. The recent US-Japan trade deal has reduced uncertainty, which may ease pressure on the BoJ.

If inflation forecasts are raised, it could give clues about future interest rates. On the downside, weak industrial production data for June may hurt growth, but this could be balanced by a rebound in retail sales.

Economic Data from Europe, UK and the US

The US will be a key focus next week thanks to a data dump and, of course, trade deal announcements.

On Wednesday, we will get 2nd quarter GDP data, which I expect to grow 3.3% (above the 2.5% forecast), driven by strong trade and investment. However, consumer spending, a key growth driver, has slowed since late 2024 due to tariff concerns and economic uncertainty.

This will be followed by the Fed rate decision later in the day. We obviously have the ongoing attacks at Fed Chair Jerome Powell by US President Trump and his administration.

However, this is unlikely to sway the Fed at this stage, as they are likely to adopt a wait and see approach. The economy is slowing but stable. The Fed is unlikely to cut rates now, though I could see a 50 bp cut in December if inflation eases. The US data week will end with a focus on Jobs data and PCE.

The Fed's preferred inflation measure, the core PCE deflator, is expected to rise 0.3% in June, slightly higher than CPI's 0.2%. NFP data on Friday is expected at 100-120k, with unemployment ticking up to 4.2%.

As Europe heads into summer, key eurozone data is due. GDP is expected to slow after a strong 1Q boosted by U.S. trade activity. April saw drops in production and exports, though May had a rebound, especially in pharmaceuticals. Overall, U.S. developments likely hurt eurozone GDP, and weak service sector performance may add to the slowdown.

On Friday we will get Euro area inflation data. ECB President Lagarde has highlighted stable inflation and steady growth as positives. July's inflation data is expected to stay calm, but the focus will be on the U.S.-EU trade relationship as the August 1 deadline nears.

Chart of the Week - Gold (XAU/USD)

This week's chart of the week is gold (XAU/USD). Gold has shrugged off its early week gains and dropped over $100 from the weekly highs around the $3440/oz mark.

Looking at the chart below, we have the triangle pattern, which appeared to experience an upside breakout earlier in the week before reversing now to test the lower band of the triangle pattern.

Gold has been mixed since making fresh all-time highs in April of $3500/oz with higher highs followed by lower lows. However, the failure this week to take out the most recent swing high at $3451/oz on June 16 may warrant caution for bulls.

A break below the lower end of the triangle pattern would usually be a sign of further downside; however, following the false breakout this week, market participants may rightly be slightly confused.

If the trendline holds, bulls may return; however, if it does give way, then the door may be opening for a larger retracement, and the $3300 level is the next key spot of support I will pay attention to.

Gold (XAU/USD) Daily Chart - July 25, 2025

Key Levels to Consider:

Support

  • 3300
  • 3278
  • 3251 (100-day MA)

Resistance

  • 3350
  • 3400
  • 3425

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: JAKKS Pacific: Caught In Tariff Turmoil (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
JAKKS Pacific, Inc. (NASDAQ:JAKK) reported concerning Q2 results – the company is understandably caught in significant tariff turbulence due to sourcing from China. The toy company previously had strong operational momentum with content-led sales growth, but such momentum is now clearly behind. As the global trade environment has been quick to change and the U.S. consumer sentiment varies, JAKKS has a very turbulent outlook ahead.

I remained at a Buy rating in my previous October 2024 article, titled “JAKKS Pacific Q3: Christmas Came Early”. The stock has since lost -38% of its value due to tariff policies, significantly underperforming S&P 500’s 12% gain.

JAKKS Is Caught in Tariff Turmoil in the U.S.

As can be expected, JAKKS reported a significant earnings decline in Q2. The report showed revenues of just $119.1 million, down by -20% year-on-year and missing Wall Street’s already quite conservative consensus by $5.0 million. Earnings also showed a considerable decline, although the still positive adjusted EPS of $0.03 beat expectations by $0.20.

The quarter reflects the impact of tariffs on JAKKS in the U.S. – Trump’s current tariffs on China have caused havoc on JAKKS’ sourcing that’s predominantly based in the country, fundamentally altering JAKKS’ earnings prospects. The current nominal 55% tariff level on Chinese goods poses a massive additional cost on JAKKS, while the effective tariff level stands at a still significant but lower 30% level due to exemptions. The trade war between the U.S. and China during the April-May period, temporarily causing triple-digit percentage tariffs, likely caused temporarily even higher operational turbulence for JAKKS in Q2.

The sales performance clearly reflects hesitancy throughout the industry as JAKKS’ U.S. sales declined by -31% to $87.0 million; in the Q2 earnings call, JAKKS already noted that pricing increases have been met with consumer resistance on the retail level. The consumer sentiment has weakened, already causing more uncertain spending as JAKKS’ retailer customers are trying to manage the tariff landscape. Pricing power and consumer behavior largely remain cloudy as JAKKS’ earnings call also reflects significant uncertainty from the management. Tariff changes have essentially put a halt on JAKKS’ previously very strong momentum, led by massive content-led sales growth in Q1. The film slate still stands strong for JAKKS, which should aid the company in maintaining sales better through the 2025-2026 period in a challenging industry environment.

Positively, JAKKS’ gross margin even expanded by 80 basis points to 32.8% in Q2 despite tariff pressure; JAKKS seems to be focused on protecting margins with pricing and instead sacrificing sales volume. Content-led sales growth was noted to cause a better sales mix, but regardless, the gross profit performance is impressively strong when considering tariffs. JAKKS has a minimal inventory level, making sourcing price increases show up fast in margins. Regardless, earnings plummeted due to massive sales deleverage, and operating income declined to a -$2.8 million loss from a $7.6 million profit in Q2’24; maintaining the gross margin still comes with a massive price tag.

The U.S. market performance has minimal visibility in upcoming quarters despite a strong film slate. Tariff policies have been fast to change, making tariff mitigation efforts challenging to execute - JAKKS has tried to mitigate tariffs by switching sourcing to Vietnam and Mexico, but both countries now also face significant tariffs as well. The U.S. and China have ongoing trade talks, but a sustainable trade deal hasn’t yet been reached. JAKKS' long-term ability to shift increased tariffs onto consumers is uncertain, albeit as a silver lining, the industry as a whole relies largely on Chinese imports; with the industry basically all facing the same cost increase, JAKKS should manage to increase prices well. At the same time, consumer uncertainty adds another layer of weakness to the current outlook.

Regardless of such uncertainty, I believe that JAKKS’ very weak Q2 doesn’t reflect the company’s long-term earnings prospects. The ability to shift tariffs into pricing should improve over time. As the U.S.-China trade war escalated during Q2, JAKKS had to put a lot of business in especially costumes on pause during the quarter, but the currently already lower tariff level should enable deliveries to resume. As tariff policies stabilize, JAKKS should be able to plan sourcing changes better for the long term. For now, earnings have to be taken one quarter at a time as the outlook is very volatile. Being a bright spot in the Q2 report, JAKKS has managed to shift sales towards international markets from the U.S. market in a major initiative as international sales surged by 41% to $32.1 million.

JAKKS’ Valuation Has Very Uncertain Upside

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for JAKKS. The DCF model now reflects significant tariff pressure, causing significant pressure on JAKKS’ fair valuation.

I assume sales weakness to continue throughout 2025, albeit at a slower pace in H2 than in Q2 with a total -8.5% decline for the year. I afterwards assume sales to stabilize at quite a subdued level with 2% perpetual growth from 2026 forward.

JAKKS’ margin outlook remains highly uncertain with ongoing tariff uncertainty. In a base scenario, I assume the EBIT margin to ultimately stabilize at 5%, 70 basis points below the 2024 level. The estimate suggests that JAKKS manages to mitigate a good portion of the tariff impact throughout the next couple of years. I still expect JAKKS to convert after-tax earnings well into free cash flow.

The estimates put JAKKS’ fair value at $24.80, 31% above the stock price at the time of writing – the stock has a good amount of upside in my base scenario. Such upside remains highly speculative though – the tariff landscape is still evolving, and JAKKS’ sustainable earnings level is very hard to gauge for now. As such, I believe that the stock should be approached with caution despite the estimated base scenario upside.

The fair value estimate is down from $42.15 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 11.39%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I don’t assume JAKKS to draw a noteworthy amount of debt. For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.42% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.62% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent July estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.40. With a liquidity premium of 0.5%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 11.39%.

Takeaway

JAKKS has gotten caught in tariff turmoil due to the company’s exposure to Chinese sourcing. Mitigation efforts have so far been challenging, and as the consumer reaction and the tariff landscape evolve, JAKKS has a very uncertain earnings outlook ahead. While the stock has significant upside potential if tariff pressure decreases, such upside is speculative for now. As such, while the stock has gone much lower, I downgrade my rating on JAKKS to Hold for now.
